core_principles: Teleology: human life has a final end (beatitude/union with God) that orders other goods
Virtue ethics: virtues habituate and perfect powers; prudence orders reason and will
Natural law/right reason: moral norms grounded in participation in eternal/divine law
Charity and grace: theological virtues perfect nature and elevate virtues toward the supernatural end
Moral psychology: passions are natural movements that must be ordered by reason and the will
Epistemology: intellect grasps self‑evident first principles and demonstrative truths within its order
attack_patterns: Appeals to teleology and natural law to expose incoherence in anti‑teleological positions
Reframes freedom as ordered will (not radical self‑creation), using prudence and charity
Targets existentialist/nihilistic and pleasure‑centric accounts (Sartre, Camus, Nietzsche, Epicurus) for lacking a final end
Combines philosophical authorities (Aristotle) and theological resources (Scripture, doctrine) in rebuttal
Concedes useful elements of opponents' positions but insists on theological/metaphysical limits
Emphasizes the common good, justice, and prudential restraint when criticizing permissiveness or laxity
defense_patterns: Reiterates the Thomistic synthesis: nature oriented to supernatural beatitude; grace perfects nature
Restates ordering of intellect, will, and passions; defends virtues and rites as formative means, not ends
Defends the primacy of natural reason for moral knowledge while placing revelation as completion
Frames political and legal claims via natural/divine law (lex iniusta non est lex) and proportionate justice
Integrates opponents' insights (e.g., value of rites, simplicity) but situates them as subordinate instruments
Deploys systematic metaphysical argumentation to absorb and deflect criticisms within his framework
strategic_strengths: High coherence and internal consistency across positions
Deep metaphysical and theological resources that supply argumentative depth
Robust, resilient defenses when debate stays within teleological/theistic premises
Effective, targeted dialectical engagement that combines authority and argument
Reliable practical vocabulary (prudence, common good, law) for political/moral disputes
typical_targets: Existentialists (e.g., Sartre, Camus)
Nietzschean will/overman rhetoric
Taoist/Lao‑Tzu quietism and permissive passivity
Stoicism and extreme detachment
Cynic asceticism (Diogenes) and purely anti‑social renunciation
Epicurean pleasure‑centrism and hedonistic reductions
Moral relativists/unjust positive law (those who deny natural law constraints)
Ritualism when rites conflict with right reason (e.g., unexamined Confucian rites)
vulnerabilities: Dependence on acceptance of theistic/metaphysical premises (teleology, God, grace)
Relatively low originality; positions often read as traditional/predictable
Less persuasive to secular, anti‑metaphysical, or radical existential interlocutors
Tendency to leave immediate practical/material policy concerns (hunger, emergency needs) less directly answered
Exposed by theodical pressures (grievous gratuitous evil) and difficulties reconciling suffering with providence
Vulnerable to psychodynamic and existential critiques about autonomy, contingency, and non‑rational sources of behavior
collapse_conditions: Interlocutors consistently reject teleology, natural law, and theistic premises as illegitimate starting points
Debate is reframed to demand novel, non‑tradition‑based solutions or empirical policy answers outside Thomistic tooling
Sustained focus on urgent material/practical cases where theological remedies appear inapplicable or insufficient
Opponents successfully secularize moral vocabulary and show that final‑end talk is eliminable for practical reasoning

core_principles: lucidity about the absurd — clear recognition of death, suffering, and the world's indifference
sustained revolt — active, continual refusal of resignation or metaphysical escape
creation of values through concrete action and solidarity rather than appeal to transcendence
refusal of suicide and transcendental consolation as legitimate responses
preservation of human dignity and limits — opposition to instrumentalizing persons
emphasis on love/solidarity as the social basis of revolt
attack_patterns: charge metaphysical/consolatory doctrines (theistic or Platonic) as evasions of the absurd
denounce withdrawal/serene resignation (Epicurean, Taoist, Stoic withdrawal) as complicity or quietism
expose doctrines that aestheticize power or abstract freedom (Nietzsche, some readings of Sartre) as legitimizing violence
criticize ritualist or duty-first systems (Confucian rites, ritual deference) as masking domination
highlight consequentialist or prudential rationalizations that permit instrumentalization or murder
defense_patterns: reframe revolt as collective, non‑violent, dignity‑preserving solidarity
couple passion/emotion with lucidity and principled limits to avoid absolutism
assert pragmatic middle-paths that radicalize obligations without endorsing metaphysical consolations
acknowledge tragic compromises while insisting on moral limits (refuse murderous certainties)
allow narrowly circumscribed prudential exceptions while warning against complicity
strategic_strengths: high coherence and consistency in framing (stable, repeatable doctrine)
strong dialectical skill — effective rhetorical attacks exposing evasions and moral costs
ethical clarity focused on human dignity and limits
ability to occupy a resilient 'middle path' between quietism and absolutism
persuasive, vivid appeals to lived experience and historical examples
typical_targets: Aquinas / theological metaphysical consolations
Epicurus / Epicurean withdrawal
Lao Tzu / Taoist resignation
Stoics / Diogenes / Marcus-style resignation
Nietzsche (will-to-power/overman)
Confucian ritualism and duty-focused orders
Sartre-style abstract/totalizing freedom
ritualist or truth-as-domination figures (e.g., Hypatia in summaries)
vulnerabilities: the notion of 'lucidity' is under-specified and can be accused of lack of normative grounding
difficulty translating revolt into stable political/institutional structures
risk of revolt escalating into new tyranny if limits are not enforceable
delicate boundary between prudential deception and complicity — hard to operationalize
relative lack of resources offered for ceremonial or social functions that rituals supply
collapse_conditions: revolt hardens into absolutism or sanctioned violence (co‑option of lucidity to justify terror)
breakdown or erosion of solidaristic bonds that sustain collective revolt
failure to institutionalize or sustain revolt as a stable political order (practical institutional collapse)
widespread demand for transcendental consolation or ritual order that undermines existential lucidity
persistent under-specification of limits so critics exploit ambiguities to delegitimize the stance

core_principles: rén (humaneness) as the highest moral aim
lǐ (rites, music, ritual) as formative practices that shape character and judgment
yì (righteousness) to correct and guide conduct within social roles
self-cultivation through study, reflection, imitation of exemplars, and sustained practice
social harmony and role‑based duty (family, polity) as the locus of meaning
political legitimacy contingent on virtue; remonstration or withdrawal are proper responses to wicked rule
attack_patterns: reframe disputes around social formation: stress rites, education, and habit over abstract individual choice
argue that unchecked individualism, revolt, or pleasure‑calculus corrodes trust and communal order
stress long‑term social consequences (erosion of rites, civic trust, Mandate of Heaven) to rebut adversaries
contrast concrete pedagogy and institutional reform with opponents' abstract or solitary claims
charge valorization of power, suffering, or novelty with producing cruelty, caprice, or social fragmentation
defense_patterns: reiterate that rites, music, example, and study reliably cultivate sincere virtue (not mere compliance)
defend rites as substantive, reformable instruments of moral education rather than empty custom
appeal to alignment with Heaven/Way and to durable social goods to meet existential and moral critiques
insist on practical remedies (remonstrance, gradual reform, withdrawal of service) rather than revolution
emphasize that disciplined practice makes right action spontaneous and restrains selfishness
strategic_strengths: high coherence and doctrinal consistency—arguments map tightly onto a stable ethical system
depth in socio‑ethical reasoning and pedagogy: concrete institutional and formative resources
effective dialectical moves against individualism, hedonism, and nihilism by shifting focus to social costs
robust defensive repertoire (rites, exemplars, remonstrance) usable against many attacks
predictable, conservative posture that secures trust with audiences valuing order and tradition
typical_targets: radical individualism/existentialism (Sartre‑type) that elevates isolated autonomy
nihilism/absurdist revolt (Camus‑type) that prizes defiant destruction of order
hedonism/utilitarian reductionism (Epicurus‑type) that collapses justice into pleasure‑calculus
anti‑ritual asceticism or cynicism (Diogenes‑type) that rejects social forms and obligations
valorization of power, suffering, or novel self‑creation (Nietzsche‑type) that threatens communal bonds
vulnerabilities: relatively low originality—positions can seem conservative or predictable
risk of ritual formalism: rites can be accused of hollowing into mere habit or bad faith
tendency to underplay systemic power, coercion, and the exigencies of brutal regimes
weaker against purely metaphysical or theological proofs (e.g., claims about soul/afterlife) outside social ethics
limited appeal to audiences prioritizing radical authenticity, creative transformation, or rapid change
collapse_conditions: when rites and institutions become hollow or ossified and lose sincerity (ritual no longer forms virtue)
under concentrated repression or brutal regimes where remonstrance and withdrawal are impossible or fatal
when opponents successfully defend individual autonomy or excellence as necessary goods that rites suppress
when debates hinge on metaphysical/theological claims about the soul or afterlife that social ethics cannot answer
when rapid social breakdown destroys the institutions (families, schools, rites) that sustain cultivation

core_principles: Virtue as the sole highest good; moral worth independent of wealth, praise, or death
Radical self-sufficiency (autarky) and voluntary poverty as routes to freedom
Shamelessness and absolute honesty as techniques to escape opinion and expose hypocrisy
Rejection of man‑made conventions, rites, and laws that serve power or vanity
Meaning forged in practice/lived example rather than metaphysics or posthumous reward
attack_patterns: Scornful, performative denunciation of opponents’ comforts, rites, or consolations
Expose hypocrisy by contrasting rhetorical claims with lived practice (practical exemplification)
Paint opposing doctrines as fostering dependency, cowardice, or vanity (e.g., hedonists, ritualists, theologians, power‑seekers)
Use blunt, provocative rhetoric and ascetic imagery to delegitimize abstract theorizing
defense_patterns: Insist on practice over theory: living example as empirical proof of claims
Reassert that laws bind only insofar as they promote virtue; otherwise they should be rejected
Frame austerity and trials as moral training (discipline rather than masochism)
Reinterpret harsh measures as corrective or 'tough compassion' aimed at producing virtue
strategic_strengths: High internal coherence and consistency across positions and replies
Rhetorical force: vivid, provocative exemplars that effectively expose perceived hypocrisy
Clear, easily communicated doctrinal core (simple maxims easily tested by action)
Dialectical effectiveness at undermining performative or comfort‑based philosophies
typical_targets: Hedonists/comfort‑seekers (Epicurean-style positions)
Ritualists/traditionalists who defend rites that mask power (Confucian-style)
Theologians/consolers who sacralize suffering or promise posthumous reward (Aquinas-style)
Philosophies valorizing power, domination, or theatrical greatness (Nietzschean variants)
Existentialist/intellectual fashions that theatricalize revolt or pity (Camus, Sartre, Hypatia)
vulnerabilities: Pragmatic impracticality: prescriptions assume rare, hard-to-achieve autarky and ignore most people's material dependence
Weakness against social/collective arguments — neglects communal goods, institutions, and solidarity
Accusations of cruelty or callousness when contempt for pity looks harmful in practice
Susceptibility to charge that plainness can be a mask or performance (failure to refute performative plainness)
Defensive fragility: defenses rely heavily on exemplarity and are less robust to structural or systemic critiques
collapse_conditions: When interlocutors foreground unavoidable embodied needs, systemic injustice, or institutional solutions that cannot be met by individual austerity
When critics demonstrate that genuine autarky is unattainable for most, making prescriptions inapplicable or elitist
When concrete cases show that radical honesty or rule‑breaking produces greater harm than the tolerated deception or compliance
When opponents successfully reframe plainness as another form of social performance or mask, undermining the credibility of lived exemplar

core_principles: Ataraxia and aponia (absence of mental disturbance and bodily pain) are the highest goods
Pleasure/pain are primary value data but must be governed by prudence/reason
Virtues (prudence, temperance, justice) are instrumentally valuable to secure tranquility
Justice is a convention/pact to prevent mutual harm and preserve peaceful conditions
Friendship is a central instrumental good that sustains long‑term tranquility
Reason/empiricism dispels false fears (esp. gods and death) and orders desires
Limit vain desires; prefer simple, necessary pleasures
attack_patterns: Prudential critique of metaphysical/religious consolations (rejects rites, providence, theological theodicies)
Target existential/revolt positions as needlessly anxious and disruptive of tranquility (e.g., Sartre, Camus)
Critique ascetic or heroic suffering as unnecessary and harmful to ataraxia (e.g., Diogenes, Stoic austerity)
Invoke atomism/naturalism (including clinamen) to undercut teleology and providential accounts
Emphasize concrete harms and practical consequences of rival doctrines over abstract ideals
defense_patterns: Reiterate justice‑as‑pact and prudential limits: obey laws when they secure tranquility, resist when they cause needless harm
Reaffirm that pleasure properly understood = freedom from pain/disturbance; virtues serve that end
Defend teachability of wisdom as therapeutic: argument, example, and friendship free from fear
Restate empiricism and atomism to justify dispelling fears of gods/death and to ground ethics
Default commitment to truthfulness, with limited, prudential exceptions when disclosure would cause greater harm
strategic_strengths: High coherence and consistency across positions (reliable doctrinal fit)
Practically oriented, persuasive prudential arguments aimed at reducing concrete suffering
Robust naturalistic/empiricist framework that deflates supernatural and teleological claims
Effective emphasis on friendship and social practices to undercut moral skepticism
Capable, repeatable defenses that reframe alleged cowardice as prudent courage
typical_targets: Existentialists emphasizing revolt and anguished value‑creation (Sartre, Camus)
Ascetic or radical individualists/anti‑social critics (Diogenes)
Stoic duty‑first figures (e.g., Marcus Aurelius)
Religious/theological apologists and metaphysicians (e.g., Aquinas)
Confucian/Taoist ritualists or mystics (Confucius, Lao‑Tzu)
Nietzschean valorization of struggle/power
vulnerabilities: Prone to charges of reductionism (reducing morality to prudential self‑interest)
May underappreciate collective, civic, or higher‑order moral goods beyond individual tranquility
Accused of retreat/cowardice or political disengagement when tranquility is prioritized over justice
Predictability and limited originality — arguments can be anticipated and labeled conventional
Dependence on empirical dispelling of fears (gods/death) and on the clinamen — metaphysical or epistemic challenges weaken the case
Risk of slippery‑slope objections when allowing prudential exceptions (e.g., truthfulness exceptions)
collapse_conditions: When tranquility/ataraxia cannot be secured (widespread political violence or social collapse) and prudential withdrawal becomes infeasible
When opponents successfully establish non‑hedonic moral demands or collective duties that require sustained sacrifice
When reason/empiricism fail to dispel fears about gods or death for the audience, leaving core motivators intact
When rivals reframe suffering as constitutive of meaning or moral growth in ways that plausibly override prudential calculus

core_principles: Neoplatonic/Platonic metaphysics: ascent to the One and participation in intelligible realities
Primacy of reason and the Nous over sense-perception
Purification/discipline of the soul through study (mathematics, dialectic) and virtue
Justice as ordering principle for soul and polis, balanced with measured mercy
Education and civic cultivation as means to moral and intellectual improvement
Truth as the proper ideal, with only narrowly defined prudential exceptions
attack_patterns: Metaphysical rebuttal: invoke intelligible order/Forms to undermine existentialist and empiricist positions
Dialectical pressure combining ethical and metaphysical critique (showing social/consequential costs of rivals' doctrines)
Appeal to civic examples, pedagogy, and historical experience to expose practical harms of opponents
Stress the need for disciplined study (arith., geometry, dialectic) to bind passions and legitimize claims
Target rival excesses (unbounded freedom, will-to-power, ritualism, passive gentleness) by highlighting social corruption or impotence
defense_patterns: Reassert the metaphysical framework (One, Nous, participation) to answer objections
Emphasize cultivation, persuasion, law and institutions as practical means rather than coercion
Frame reason as enabling and refining desires, not as tyrannical repression
Allow narrowly bounded prudential exceptions (rare silence or deception) while defending truth as the norm
Offer multi‑pronged reforms (education, argument, measured deterrence) to preserve polis and intellect
strategic_strengths: High coherence and consistency across positions
Strong dialectical skill: incisive, historically informed critiques
Deep metaphysical resources that provide argumentative depth and resilience within tradition
Effective civic/pedagogical framing that links theory to social practice
Robust defensive repertoire combining principle and limited practical concessions
typical_targets: Existentialists and radical freedom doctrines (e.g., Sartre, Camus)
Nihilistic or domination-focused positions (e.g., Nietzschean will-to-power)
Ritualists and unexamined tradition (e.g., ritual/ceremonial conservatism, Confucianism as mere rite)
Hedonists/atomists who privilege sense-perception (e.g., Epicureanism)
Religious or theological positions that subordinate reason to revelation or clerical authority (e.g., Aquinas-style fideism)
vulnerabilities: Dependence on contested metaphysical premises makes arguments weak against materialists/naturalists
Perceived elitism or lack of empirical/practical grounding in addressing immediate bodily suffering
Risk of doctrinal inconsistency when making prudential concessions (undermining absolutist rhetoric)
Less originality; arguments can read as traditional/conservative rather than novel
May seem dismissive of experiential or existential claims, weakening appeal to some audiences
collapse_conditions: Interlocutors reject metaphysical premises and demand strictly empirical, materialist demonstrations
Situations of acute social breakdown or emergency where education, law, and persuasion are unavailable or ineffective
Powerful emotional, political, or populist appeals that sideline reason and civic pedagogy
Compounding systemic injustice and immediate bodily suffering that outpace slow, educative remedies
Internal tensions exposed by concessions (when exceptions proliferate and doctrinal coherence erodes)

core_principles: wu-wei (non-striving/non-action as skillful, non-coercive responsiveness)
ziran/spontaneity (action arising from naturalness/the uncarved root)
simplicity and emptying desire (return to plainness as moral/epistemic cure)
softness/compassion as strength (water metaphor: yielding defeats force)
skepticism about fixed names and overconceptualization (ineffability of the Way)
governance by example and minimal, light law
attack_patterns: diagnose opponents as 'forcing' or clinging—portray their methods as generating bondage
use evocative natural metaphors (water, uncarved wood, root) to displace rivals' frameworks
redirect debates from abstract justification to practical harmony and removal of causes
target rites, rigid duties, will-driven virtue, and calculative pleasure-seeking as counterproductive
rhetorical, poetic critiques that emphasize prudence and preservation over analytic refutation
frequently reframe strength so opponents' notions of force or heroic striving appear self-defeating
defense_patterns: restate and clarify wu-wei as active responsiveness rather than cowardice or mere passivity
reaffirm metaphors (water, yielding, uncarved block) to show durability of the stance
emphasize inner cultivation practices (emptying desire, returning to simplicity) as practical method
defend minimal governance—use law sparingly and govern by example to restore harmony
repeat core premises to defuse critiques (non-action dissolves knots of strife) rather than engage in fine-grained analytic rebuttal
strategic_strengths: high internal coherence and consistency (doctrinally unified worldview)
philosophical depth and evocative originality (memorable metaphors and imagery)
effective at redirecting disputes away from opponents' premises toward alternative praxis
persuasive as a holistic, practice-oriented account of virtue and politics
moderately strong dialectical attack (able to expose perceived excesses in rival positions)
typical_targets: Confucian ritualism / elaborate rites
Stoic will-driven duty (e.g., Marcus-style prescriptive striving)
Nietzschean will-to-power / valorization of struggle
Epicurean/calculative pleasure-seeking
revolutionary or heroic revolt (Camus, Diogenes-style confrontationalism)
rule- and law-centered theological or legalist accounts (e.g., Aquinas-style structured norms)
existentialist self-fashioning that prizes strenuous self-creation (e.g., Sartre)
vulnerabilities: seen as passive or insufficient in urgent moral/political crises (accused of inaction)
vagueness—offers few precise criteria or procedural rules for complex cases
relies on metaphysical premises (the Tao/ineffability) that opponents can reject
repetitive defense strategy (reiteration of premises) leaves gaps against demands for analytic precision
may underplay threats from unrepentant tyrants or actors who will not be swayed by softness
collapse_conditions: situations demanding decisive, coercive intervention where non-action increases harm
facing agents or regimes that exploit softness and do not respond to governing-by-example
debates that require clear, rule-like criteria, measurable outcomes, or stepwise procedures
audiences that reject metaphysical/ineffability premises and demand empirical or normative justification
sustained practical pressure (protracted crises) that expose vagueness or lack of concrete policy tools

core_principles: Reason/Logos as the ruling faculty that must govern impressions and actions
Virtue (living according to Nature and reason) as the sole true good
Clear distinction between what is up to us (judgment/assent) and externals (indifferents)
Withholding assent until impressions are clear/kataleptic; disciplined judgment
Acceptance of fate and mortality (memento mori) as motivation for right action
Duty to family, polis, and the common good; cosmopolitan justice
Truthfulness preferred; silence to avoid falsehood
Moral formation through habituation, training, and example
attack_patterns: Stress the social dangers of passion, pride, and self‑creation; portray opponents as socially disruptive
Argue that pleasure‑seeking or withdrawal (Epicurus, Lao Tzu) neglects duty and harms the commonwealth
Charge existential self‑fashioning (Sartre, Nietzsche) with pride, instability, or domination
Use practical, civic examples and mortality to pressure opponents into showing real‑world consequences
Contrast disciplined reason and order with rivals' perceived passivity, excess, or sentimentalism
defense_patterns: Reiterate the up/down control: govern impressions, assent only to kataleptic impressions
Frame acceptance as active, disciplined agency rather than passive resignation
Emphasize virtue's sufficiency and the instrumental status of externals and rites
Defend truthfulness and silence as morally superior to expedient falsehoods
Point to habituation, training, and tested practice as the means of securing right judgment
strategic_strengths: High coherence and internal consistency across positions
Effective, practically oriented dialectical attacks focused on civic consequences
Robust, repeatable defenses that reframe passivity as disciplined action
Psychologically persuasive rhetoric (memento mori, duty, self‑command)
Operationalizable program (habituation, rites as instruments) for moral formation
typical_targets: Sartrean existentialism (radical self‑creation and subjectivity)
Epicurean hedonism and pleasure‑centric ethics
Taoist/Lao Tzu passivity or withdrawal
Nietzschean glorification of power/self‑aggrandizement
Camusian revolt/absurdism
Confucian ritualism when rites lack inward assent or justice
vulnerabilities: Relatively low originality; predictable Stoic repertory
Portrayals of passivity or emotional coldness invite charge of inflexibility
Vulnerable to genealogical/skeptical critiques that reason and assent are shaped by drives
Reliance on kataleptic impressions invites skeptical doubts about perceptual reliability
Brittleness in hard moral dilemmas (truth vs. life, silence vs. preventing harm)
Can underplay the moral need for transformative, collective resistance against systemic injustice
collapse_conditions: Widespread systemic injustice or oppression that clearly demands robust, collective resistance rather than measured assent
Successful undermining of the neutrality/reliability of reason or kataleptic impressions (Nietzschean genealogy or radical skepticism)
Opponents reframing moral authority around creative self‑valuation or existential authenticity that makes duty claims less persuasive
Situations where strict truthfulness or silence predictably causes catastrophic harm, forcing tradeoffs the doctrine lacks machinery to resolve
Mass political crises requiring passionate mobilization rather than tranquil, individualized discipline

core_principles: Will to power as fundamental drive and value‑generator
Amor fati / eternal return: affirmative embrace of fate and suffering
Perspectivism: truth as contingent, interpretive, instrumentally judged by life‑affirmation
Self‑overcoming and creative self‑formation (becoming an artist of oneself)
Revaluation of values: rejection of herd/ascetic morality, pity, and otherworldly consolation
Rejection of metaphysical absolutes (sovereign Truth, juridical free will)
attack_patterns: Psychological/genealogical diagnosis: exposing opponents' doctrines as life‑denying or rooted in ressentiment
Polemical, rhetorically forceful denunciation of pity, asceticism, and herd morality
Reframing debates around life‑affirmation and creative greatness rather than abstract duty or consolation
Targeting purported weaknesses: labeling tranquility/prudence/quietism as cowardice or palliation
Occasional ad hominem or theatrical overreach to delegitimize rivals' motives and values
defense_patterns: Doubling down on will‑to‑power, perspectivism, and amor fati as positive programs (value‑creation)
Distinguishing noble/creative strength from petty cruelty; portraying generosity as overflow of strength
Recasting critiques of truth, duty, and free will as emancipatory and life‑affirming
Emphasizing disciplined self‑overcoming and aesthetic formation rather than mere domination
Persistent insistence that external duties and consolations mask decadence and hinder greatness
strategic_strengths: High rhetorical and dialectical force: incisive, memorable polemic that reframes issues on Nietzsche's terms
Internal coherence and consistency around a compact doctrinal core (will to power, perspectivism, amor fati)
Ability to expose hidden psychological motives and commonalities across rival positions
Depth and originality in revaluating values and offering an affirmative alternative to ascetic/otherworldly views
Effective at shifting normative standards from abstract duties to life‑affirmation metrics
typical_targets: Christian/medieval/ascetic morality (Aquinas, priestly otherworldliness)
Compassion/pity and egalitarian moralizing (Camus, sentimental humanism, certain Sartrean moves)
Quietist/tranquillizing traditions (Epicurus, Lao Tzu, Stoicism/Marcus) portrayed as life‑denying
Claims to metaphysical certainty and juridical free will (objectivist epistemologies, absolute Truth)
Rituals and social practices that preserve mediocrity or herd petrification (Confucian/communal conservatism)
vulnerabilities: Tendency toward perceived elitism, legitimation of domination, or cruelty—invites charges of tyranny
Practical/social consequences under‑addressed: risks to social cohesion, trust, and basic security
Perspectivism/self‑undermining objection: the doctrine can be used to attack its own claims
Reliance on rhetorical overreach and caricature can weaken credibility against careful interlocutors
Lack of concrete normative criteria beyond 'life‑affirmation' invites accusations of arbitrariness
collapse_conditions: When opponents force sustained focus on social harms, collective stability, or empirical consequences of policy
When critics demand non‑perspectival, public criteria for adjudicating conflicting values (practical normative guidance)
When perspectivist claims are turned reflexively on Nietzschean claims, exposing self‑referential incoherence
When polemic is exposed as caricature or ad hominem and audiences require rigor over rhetorical force
When emphasis on higher types and overflow generosity fails to specify who qualifies or how social costs are justified

core_principles: Radical freedom: existence precedes essence; agents constitute values by choosing projects
Absolute responsibility: choices must be owned and are consequential (one 'legislates' for humanity)
Bad faith diagnosis: evasions (rituals, teleology, tranquility, habit) are forms of self‑deception
Authenticity as moral ideal: act only by freely endorsed projects that preserve self‑ and other‑freedom
Reason is instrumental: clarifies means but cannot supply ultimate ends or metaphysical grounding
Anguish/facticity: contingency and mortality disclose freedom but do not supply meaning
Values arise in praxis: concrete projects and solidarity instantiate moral claims, not abstract goods
attack_patterns: Recast opponents as committing bad faith (showing their doctrines as evasions of freedom)
Target appeals to transcendence/essence/teleology as smuggling in pre‑given goods
Attack rituals, tradition, or tranquil dispositions as role‑enforcing escapes from responsibility
Charge reductionisms (habit, drives, prudential calculation) with denying authentic project‑making
Press opponents on ownership and concrete consequences of their claims—force praxis and solidarity tests
Use phenomenological/lived examples to expose hypocrisy or conceptual gaps
defense_patterns: Reiterate core existential claims (freedom, responsibility, ownership) as the default answer
Insist the moral test is whether actions are freely owned (e.g., 'did I own the lie and its consequences?')
Allow instrumental use of reason, law, rites only when freely endorsed as projects
Emphasize immediate certainty of consciousness and the primacy of praxis over metaphysical proofs
Frame virtues and institutions as valid only insofar as they are freely chosen and expand freedom
strategic_strengths: High coherence and internal consistency across positions (metrics support strong doctrinal unity)
Powerful dialectical moves: consistently reframes debates around bad faith and responsibility
Effective at exposing metaphysical consolations and complacent practices as evasions
Resilient rhetorical defense that redirects critiques to ownership and praxis
Frequent use of concrete examples to dramatize failures of opposing views
typical_targets: Teleological/essentialist doctrines (appeals to God, Nature, immutable Goods)
Ritualists and tradition‑based systems that prescribe roles or rites without endorsement
Schools valorizing tranquility, resignation, or consolatory accounts of death (Stoics, Epicureans, some Camus readings)
Reductionist accounts that explain ethics as habit, drives, or prudential calculation
vulnerabilities: Limited positive normative guidance—offers few systematic institutional prescriptions
Abstractness and potential lack of practical detail for social cohesion or policy
Susceptibility to deterministic, empirical, or psychological rebuttals that constrain freedom
Tendency to underplay unconscious/embodied determinants and socio‑structural limits on choice
Risk that stressing ownership alone fails to ground intersubjective norms or long‑term institutions
collapse_conditions: When opponents supply convincing intersubjective or institutional mechanisms that secure freedom without being bad‑faith evasions
When empirical or structural evidence shows socio‑economic, psychological, or biological constraints severely limit genuine choice
When formation/habituation accounts are shown compatible with authentic endorsement (weakening the bad‑faith charge)
When rites, laws, or traditions demonstrably instantiate and sustain others' freedom rather than merely imposing roles
